title: Solid-State Relay (SSR) Mains Load Switching
content: A solid-state relay uses an internal opto-isolator and TRIAC (AC) or MOSFET (DC) to switch mains-voltage loads from a low-voltage control signal (3–32 V DC). Zero-crossing circuitry fires the TRIAC only when the AC waveform crosses 0 V, minimising inrush current and EMI.
extended_content: SSR advantages over mechanical relays: no moving parts, silent operation, ≥ 500 000 switching cycles, ≤ 1 ms turn-on time, and zero-crossing turn-on reduces harmonics. Typical AC SSR (Fotek SSR-40DA): 24–380 V AC output, 40 A peak, 3–32 V DC control input (LED-current-limited opto). Heat generation: P_heat ≈ 1.5 × I_load (W); a heatsink is mandatory above ~5 A. For inductive loads (motors, contactors) derate by 30 % and add an RC snubber across the output. Failure mode: SSRs can fail closed (shorted TRIAC) — always include a mechanical disconnect or circuit breaker upstream for safety.
formula: P_dissipated ≈ 1.5 × I_load  [W, for Fotek-type TRIAC SSR]
source: Fotek SSR-40DA Datasheet; IEC 60950-1 Mains Isolation Requirements
